1:
"For every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men in things pertaining to God, that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins:" The witnesses note the general office and work of a high priest. Chrysostom notes Paul wishes to show that this covenant is far better than the old, and does so by first laying down remote considerations, since the new had nothing bodily to show — no temple, no Holy of Holies, no priest with great apparel. The witnesses note Paul defining that every high priest is taken from among men, ordained for men in things pertaining to God, to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins; the general nature of the priestly office as the standard for Christ — that Paul, beginning to set forth Christ's priesthood, first lays down what a high priest in general is: he is "taken from among men" (one of the human race, sharing their nature), and "ordained for men in things pertaining to God" (appointed to act on men's behalf in the things of God's worship and service), the chief part of his work being "that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins" — to present offerings and sacrifices to God for the sins of the people; so that the very nature of the office requires that the priest be a man, acting Godward for men, in the matter of sin and sacrifice; ordained for men in things pertaining to God, that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins. The witnesses note every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men in things pertaining to God. The witnesses note that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins.

The witnesses note the general office of a high priest — taken from among men, ordained for men in things pertaining to God, to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins; the standard for Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the general nature of the priestly office. The lesson the expositors draw is that a high priest is a man appointed to act Godward for men, offering sacrifices for sins. The witnesses note the taken from men: "every high priest taken from among men" — Chrysostom notes Paul sets forth the office; the taken from men; the the human race; the their nature; the one of them; the man. The expositors observe the ordained for men: "is ordained for men in things pertaining to God" — appointed to act on men's behalf Godward; the ordained for men; the on men's behalf; the Godward; the the worship; the appointed. The witnesses press the offer gifts: "that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices" — to present offerings to God; the offer gifts; the sacrifices; the offerings; the to God; the present. The expositors draw the lesson on the for sins: "for sins" — for the sins of the people; the for sins; the the people; the the sins; the atone; the offer. The witnesses observe the must be a man: the office requires the priest be a man; the must be a man; the the office; the a man; the human; the the nature. The expositors note the standard for Christ: this general nature is the standard for Christ's priesthood; the standard for Christ; the the office; the Christ; the the standard; the priesthood. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men in things pertaining to God, the that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins, and the priestly office. The verse thus records the general nature of the priestly office — teaching that a high priest is a man appointed to act Godward for men, offering sacrifices for sins.
2:
"Who can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way; for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity." The witnesses note that the priest's own weakness fits him to feel for the erring. Gill notes such a high priest can have compassion on those who have committed sins of ignorance and bring their sacrifices; he does not insult or upbraid them, nor break into anger, but pities and sympathizes, having a just measure of compassion suitable to their condition. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the high priest can have compassion on the ignorant and erring, since he himself is compassed with infirmity; the priest's fellow-feeling born of his own weakness — that Paul names a needful quality of the priest: he "can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way" (he can deal gently and feel measured pity for those who sin through ignorance or who stray), and the reason lies in his own condition: "for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity" — being himself a weak, sinful man, surrounded by the same frailties, he is the better able to feel for the failings of others; so that the earthly priest's very weakness fitted him to sympathize, a thing answered in Christ (who shared our infirmity, yet without sin); who can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way. The witnesses note who can have compassion on the ignorant, and on them that are out of the way. The witnesses note for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity.

The witnesses note the priest's fellow-feeling born of his own weakness — able to feel for the ignorant and erring because he himself is compassed with the same infirmity.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's compassion born of weakness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the priest's own infirmity fits him to feel measured pity for those who sin through ignorance or stray. The witnesses note the compassion: "who can have compassion on the ignorant" — Gill notes he pities, not upbraids, the erring; the compassion; the pities; the not upbraid; the the ignorant; the gentle. The expositors observe the out of the way: "and on them that are out of the way" — and those who stray; the out of the way; the stray; the wander; the the erring; the gentle. The witnesses press the his own infirmity: "for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity" — he is himself weak and frail; the his own infirmity; the weak; the frail; the compassed; the sinful. The expositors draw the lesson on the the better able: his weakness makes him better able to feel for others; the the better able; the feel for others; the their failings; the sympathize; the weak. The witnesses observe the measured pity: a just measure of compassion suitable to their condition; the measured pity; the just measure; the suitable; the their condition; the compassion. The expositors note the answered in Christ: this is answered in Christ, who shared our infirmity yet without sin; the answered in Christ; the shared infirmity; the without sin; the Christ; the sympathize. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who can have compassion on the ignorant and on them that are out of the way, the for that he himself also is compassed with infirmity, and the priest's compassion. The verse thus records the priest's compassion born of weakness — teaching that the priest's own infirmity fits him to feel measured pity for those who sin through ignorance or stray.
3:
"And by reason hereof he ought, as for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins." The witnesses note that the earthly priest must offer for his own sins too. Barnes notes that because he is a sinner — an imperfect man — as for the people, so also for himself, he must make an expiation for sins; he needs the same atonement, offering the sacrifice for himself which he does for others. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, by reason of his own infirmity, the priest ought to offer for sins as for the people, so also for himself; the earthly priest's own sinfulness requiring sacrifice for himself — that Paul draws out the consequence of the priest's infirmity (v.2): "by reason hereof" — because he too is a sinner, compassed with weakness — "he ought, as for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins"; the earthly high priest, being himself guilty, had to present a sin-offering for his own sins before he could act for the people (as on the Day of Atonement); the witnesses note this as a point of real inferiority in the Aaronic priest, marking the contrast with Christ, who, being sinless, needed no offering for himself, but offered only for the people; as for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins. The witnesses note he ought, as for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins. The witnesses note as for the people, so also for himself.

The witnesses note the earthly priest's own sinfulness requiring sacrifice for himself — guilty, he must offer for his own sins as well as the people's; a contrast with the sinless Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's own sin requiring sacrifice for himself. The lesson the expositors draw is that the earthly priest, being himself guilty, had to offer for his own sins, unlike the sinless Christ. The witnesses note the by reason hereof: "by reason hereof" — Barnes notes because he too is a sinner; the by reason hereof; the a sinner; the imperfect; the guilty; the weak. The expositors observe the for himself: "as for the people, so also for himself" — he must offer for himself too; the for himself; the for the people; the both; the his own sins; the offer. The witnesses press the to offer for sins: "to offer for sins" — to make expiation for sins; the to offer for sins; the expiation; the atonement; the the offering; the sin. The expositors draw the lesson on the Day of Atonement: the priest offered for his own sins, as on the Day of Atonement; the Day of Atonement; the his own sins; the the priest; the offered; the first. The witnesses observe the real inferiority: this is a real inferiority of the Aaronic priest; the real inferiority; the the Aaronic priest; the inferior; the guilty; the contrast. The expositors note the contrast with Christ: the contrast with Christ, who needed no offering for himself; the contrast with Christ; the sinless; the no offering; the for himself; the only the people. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he ought as for the people so also for himself to offer for sins, the as for the people so also for himself, and the priest's own sin. The verse thus records the priest's own sin requiring sacrifice — teaching that the earthly priest, being himself guilty, had to offer for his own sins, unlike the sinless Christ.
4:
"And no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron." The witnesses note that the priestly honour must come by divine call, not self-appointment. Gill notes the office of the high priest was a very honourable one — a peculiar honour to Aaron and his sons to be separated to it, their instalment grand and solemn, anointed with oil and clothed with glorious garments. The witnesses note Paul declaring that no man takes the priestly honour to himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron; the priesthood a divine calling, not a self-assumed honour — that Paul names a second requisite of the true priest: the office is "this honour" (a high and sacred dignity), and "no man taketh" it "unto himself" — none may presume to thrust himself into it by his own will — "but he that is called of God, as was Aaron," for the priesthood must rest on a divine appointment, even as Aaron was expressly called and consecrated by God; so that lawful priesthood depends not on self-assumption but on God's own call, a principle that prepares the way to show that Christ too was divinely called to his office; no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron. The witnesses note no man taketh this honour unto himself. The witnesses note but he that is called of God, as was Aaron.

The witnesses note the priesthood as a divine calling — no man takes the honour to himself, but only he called of God, as Aaron; lawful priesthood resting on God's appointment.

So the witnesses make the verse the priesthood as a divine calling. The lesson the expositors draw is that the priestly office rests not on self-assumption but on God's own call, as with Aaron. The witnesses note the honour: "no man taketh this honour unto himself" — Gill notes the priesthood a high, sacred dignity; the honour; the the priesthood; the dignity; the sacred; the high. The expositors observe the none presume: none may thrust himself into it by his own will; the none presume; the not himself; the by his will; the not thrust; the unlawful. The witnesses press the called of God: "but he that is called of God" — only he called of God; the called of God; the divine call; the appointed; the God's call; the lawful. The expositors draw the lesson on the as Aaron: "as was Aaron" — as Aaron was expressly called and consecrated; the as Aaron; the called; the consecrated; the expressly; the by God. The witnesses observe the divine appointment: lawful priesthood rests on divine appointment; the divine appointment; the rests on; the God's call; the lawful; the not self. The expositors note the prepares for Christ: this prepares to show Christ too was divinely called; the prepares for Christ; the Christ; the divinely called; the his office; the prepares. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the no man taketh this honour unto himself, the but he that is called of God as was Aaron, and the divine calling. The verse thus records the priesthood as a divine calling — teaching that the priestly office rests not on self-assumption but on God's own call, as with Aaron.
5:
"So also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest; but he that said unto him, Thou art my Son, to day have I begotten thee." The witnesses note that Christ too was appointed priest by the Father, not himself. Gill notes it was a glorifying of Christ to make him an high priest — not as God, for as such no addition can be made to his glory, but as man; it was an honour to the human nature to be united to the Son of God. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ glorified not himself to be made high priest, but was appointed by him that said, "Thou art my Son, today have I begotten thee"; Christ's priesthood received from the Father, not self-assumed — that Paul applies the principle to Christ: "so also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest" — he did not, of his own will, presume to take the priestly office, but received it from the Father, even as Aaron was called — and the One who appointed him is "he that said unto him, Thou art my Son, to day have I begotten thee" (the Father, who in the Psalm declared his eternal Sonship); so that the same God who owned Christ as his Son also constituted him High Priest, the priesthood resting, as it must, on the Father's call and not on self-assumption; he that said unto him, Thou art my Son, to day have I begotten thee. The witnesses note so also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest. The witnesses note he that said unto him, Thou art my Son, to day have I begotten thee.

The witnesses note Christ's priesthood received from the Father, not self-assumed — appointed by him who said "Thou art my Son"; the same God who owned him as Son made him High Priest.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's priesthood received from the Father. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ did not take the priestly office himself, but received it from the Father who owned him as Son. The witnesses note the glorified not himself: "so also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest" — Christ did not presume to take the office; the glorified not himself; the not presume; the not take; the the office; the received. The expositors observe the as man: Gill notes it was an honour to the human nature; the as man; the the human nature; the honour; the united; the the Son. The witnesses press the appointed by the Father: he received it from the Father, as Aaron was called; the appointed by the Father; the received; the from the Father; the as Aaron; the called. The expositors draw the lesson on the Thou art my Son: "he that said unto him, Thou art my Son" — the Father who declared his Sonship; the Thou art my Son; the the Father; the Sonship; the the Psalm; the declared. The witnesses observe the today begotten: "to day have I begotten thee" — the eternal Sonship declared; the today begotten; the eternal; the Sonship; the begotten; the declared. The expositors note the Son and Priest: the same God who owned him as Son made him High Priest; the Son and Priest; the the same God; the owned as Son; the made Priest; the appointed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the so also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest, the he that said unto him Thou art my Son to day have I begotten thee, and the priesthood from the Father. The verse thus records Christ's priesthood received from the Father — teaching that Christ did not take the office himself, but received it from the Father who owned him as Son.
6:
"As he saith also in another place, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec." The witnesses note Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchisedec. Barnes notes that here, as the apostle means to be understood as saying, the same God who declared him to be his Son also said, "Thou art a priest for ever," citing Psalm 110. The witnesses note Paul citing that God said to Christ, "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec"; the everlasting priesthood of Christ on a higher order than Aaron's — that Paul adds a second Scripture to prove Christ's divine call to the priesthood, and one that lifts it above the Aaronic: God says to him, "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec" — not after the order of Aaron (a changing, dying, repeated priesthood) but after the order of Melchizedek, that mysterious king-priest of Genesis; the witnesses note the two great notes here — Christ's priesthood is "for ever" (unending, never passing to a successor) and "after the order of Melchisedec" (of a higher, royal, and abiding kind), a theme Paul will expound at length, marking Christ's priesthood as far surpassing the Levitical; Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec. The witnesses note Thou art a priest for ever. The witnesses note after the order of Melchisedec.

The witnesses note Christ's everlasting priesthood after the order of Melchisedec — not Aaron's changing, dying order, but a higher, royal, abiding kind that never passes to a successor.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's priesthood on the higher order of Melchisedec. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ's priesthood is everlasting and of a higher order than Aaron's, after Melchizedek. The witnesses note the cited: "as he saith also in another place" — Barnes notes the same God cites Psalm 110; the cited; the the same God; the Psalm 110; the another place; the said. The expositors observe the a priest: "Thou art a priest for ever" — God calls him a priest; the a priest; the called; the appointed; the the office; the priest. The witnesses press the for ever: "for ever" — unending, never passing to a successor; the for ever; the unending; the never passes; the no successor; the everlasting. The expositors draw the lesson on the order of Melchisedec: "after the order of Melchisedec" — of a higher, royal, abiding kind; the order of Melchisedec; the higher; the royal; the abiding; the not Aaron. The witnesses observe the not Aaron: not the changing, dying Aaronic order; the not Aaron; the changing; the dying; the repeated; the Levitical. The expositors note the surpassing: this marks Christ's priesthood as surpassing the Levitical; the surpassing; the the Levitical; the higher; the abiding; the surpasses. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Thou art a priest for ever, the after the order of Melchisedec, and the everlasting priesthood. The verse thus records Christ's priesthood on the higher order of Melchisedec — teaching that Christ's priesthood is everlasting and of a higher order than Aaron's.
7:
"Who in the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears unto him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared;" The witnesses note Christ's agonizing prayers in the days of his flesh. Gill notes "the days of his flesh" is the time of his humiliation, when he was attended with the sinless infirmities of the human nature; it may take in his whole life on earth, but especially the time of his sufferings. The witnesses note Paul recalling that Christ, in the days of his flesh, offered prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears to him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared; the High Priest's agonizing prayers and reverent submission heard by the Father — that Paul shows how truly Christ shared our infirmity and learned obedience, pointing to his agony (chiefly in Gethsemane): "in the days of his flesh" (his earthly, suffering life) he "offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears" — earnest, anguished pleadings — "unto him that was able to save him from death" (to the Father, who could deliver him), and "was heard in that he feared"; the witnesses note this last phrase taken as meaning he was heard because of his reverent, godly fear and submission — the Father heard and answered him, not by sparing him death, but by strengthening him through it and raising him from it; offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears. The witnesses note prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears. The witnesses note unto him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared.

The witnesses note Christ's agonizing prayers in the days of his flesh — strong crying and tears to the Father who could save him from death, heard for his reverent, godly fear.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's agonizing prayers heard by the Father. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, in his earthly agony, prayed with strong crying and was heard for his reverent submission. The witnesses note the days of his flesh: "who in the days of his flesh" — Gill notes the time of his humiliation and suffering; the days of his flesh; the humiliation; the suffering; the earthly life; the infirmity. The expositors observe the prayers and tears: "prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears" — earnest, anguished pleadings; the prayers and tears; the strong crying; the anguished; the earnest; the Gethsemane. The witnesses press the able to save: "unto him that was able to save him from death" — to the Father who could deliver him; the able to save; the the Father; the deliver; the from death; the could. The expositors draw the lesson on the was heard: "and was heard in that he feared" — heard for his reverent, godly fear; the was heard; the reverent fear; the godly fear; the submission; the answered. The witnesses observe the not by sparing: heard not by sparing him death, but strengthening him through it; the not by sparing; the through it; the strengthened; the raised; the answered. The expositors note the shared infirmity: this shows how truly Christ shared our infirmity; the shared infirmity; the truly; the our infirmity; the the agony; the learned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears, the unto him that was able to save him from death and was heard in that he feared, and the agonizing prayers. The verse thus records Christ's agonizing prayers heard by the Father — teaching that Christ, in his earthly agony, prayed with strong crying and was heard for his reverent submission.
8:
"Though he were a Son, yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered;" The witnesses note that Christ, though the Son, learned obedience through suffering. Gill notes Christ is "a Son," the Son of God — not by creation, adoption, or office, but by nature, being the only begotten of the Father, having the same nature and perfections with him. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, though he were a Son, Christ learned obedience by the things which he suffered; the divine Son experientially learning obedience through suffering — that Paul marks the wonder of Christ's humiliation: "though he were a Son" (the eternal, natural Son of God, who might have claimed exemption from suffering), "yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered"; the witnesses note this does not mean Christ was ever disobedient or had to be taught to obey, but that he came to know obedience by experience — he, who as God commanded, now as man practised obedience in the hard school of suffering, learning experimentally what it is to obey God's will through pain and trial; so that the Son, in his manhood, was perfected in the actual experience of submissive obedience to the Father; though he were a Son, yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered. The witnesses note though he were a Son. The witnesses note yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered.

The witnesses note the divine Son learning obedience through suffering — not that he was ever disobedient, but that he came to know obedience by experience in the hard school of suffering.

So the witnesses make the verse the Son experientially learning obedience. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, the eternal Son, came to know obedience by experience through what he suffered. The witnesses note the a Son: "though he were a Son" — Gill notes the eternal, natural Son of God; the a Son; the eternal; the natural; the the only begotten; the divine. The expositors observe the might have claimed: who might have claimed exemption from suffering; the might have claimed; the exemption; the suffering; the the Son; the humbled. The witnesses press the learned obedience: "yet learned he obedience" — he came to know obedience by experience; the learned obedience; the by experience; the came to know; the experimentally; the obedience. The expositors draw the lesson on the not disobedient: not that he was ever disobedient or had to be taught; the not disobedient; the never; the not taught; the not need; the always obeyed. The witnesses observe the by suffering: "by the things which he suffered" — in the hard school of suffering; the by suffering; the the hard school; the pain; the trial; the suffered. The expositors note the perfected: the Son in his manhood was perfected in submissive obedience; the perfected; the his manhood; the submissive; the obedience; the experience. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the though he were a Son, the yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered, and the Son learning obedience. The verse thus records the Son experientially learning obedience — teaching that Christ, the eternal Son, came to know obedience by experience through what he suffered.
9:
"And being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him;" The witnesses note that the perfected Christ became the author of eternal salvation. Barnes notes "being made perfect" means being made a complete Saviour — a Saviour fitted in all respects to redeem men — by his sufferings. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, being made perfect, Christ became the author of eternal salvation to all them that obey him; the perfected Saviour the source of eternal salvation for the obedient — that Paul shows the glorious issue of Christ's sufferings and learned obedience: "being made perfect" (not that he had any imperfection, but being completed and consummated as a Saviour, his obedience and sacrifice finished, and himself glorified) "he became the author of eternal salvation" (the cause, source, and procurer of a salvation that is everlasting, not temporal like the deliverances of the law) — "unto all them that obey him"; the witnesses note this last as marking the objects of this salvation — those who, by faith, obey him, the obedience of faith being the mark of those who share the eternal salvation Christ has authored; he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him. The witnesses note being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation. The witnesses note unto all them that obey him.

The witnesses note the perfected Christ the author of eternal salvation — completed as a Saviour through suffering, the source of everlasting salvation for all who obey him by faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the perfected Saviour as the source of eternal salvation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, completed through suffering, is the author of everlasting salvation for the obedient. The witnesses note the made perfect: "and being made perfect" — Barnes notes made a complete Saviour through suffering; the made perfect; the complete Saviour; the completed; the consummated; the through suffering. The expositors observe the no imperfection: not that he had any imperfection, but was completed as Saviour; the no imperfection; the completed; the as Saviour; the finished; the consummated. The witnesses press the author: "he became the author of eternal salvation" — the cause, source, and procurer of salvation; the author; the the cause; the the source; the procurer; the salvation. The expositors draw the lesson on the eternal: "eternal salvation" — a salvation everlasting, not temporal; the eternal; the everlasting; the not temporal; the salvation; the abiding. The witnesses observe the them that obey: "unto all them that obey him" — those who by faith obey him; the them that obey; the by faith; the obey; the the objects; the obedient. The expositors note the obedience of faith: the obedience of faith marks those who share it; the obedience of faith; the the mark; the share; the the obedient; the faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the being made perfect he became the author of eternal salvation, the unto all them that obey him, and the perfected Saviour. The verse thus records the perfected Saviour as the source of eternal salvation — teaching that Christ, completed through suffering, is the author of everlasting salvation for the obedient.
10:
"Called of God an high priest after the order of Melchisedec." The witnesses note Christ's designation as high priest after Melchisedec's order. Gill notes there is a resemblance between Melchizedek and Christ; many things said of the one agree with the other, in his person and in what is said of him — that he was without father and mother, without beginning or end, abiding a priest continually. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ was called of God a high priest after the order of Melchisedec; the divine designation of Christ to the Melchizedek priesthood — that Paul gathers up the argument of the chapter by repeating Christ's title: he was "called of God an high priest" (divinely designated to the office, as required) "after the order of Melchisedec" (not after Aaron, but after that higher, royal, and abiding order); the witnesses note this verse returns to and seals the theme of v.6, marking Christ as the God-appointed High Priest of the superior Melchizedek order, in whom the resemblances to that ancient king-priest are fulfilled — a theme Paul is eager to expound, but must first rebuke his readers' dullness before he can; called of God an high priest after the order of Melchisedec. The witnesses note called of God an high priest. The witnesses note after the order of Melchisedec.

The witnesses note Christ's divine designation as high priest after Melchisedec's order — the God-appointed priest of the higher, royal, abiding order, in whom Melchizedek's resemblances are fulfilled.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's designation to the Melchizedek priesthood. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ was divinely called to the high priesthood after the superior order of Melchizedek. The witnesses note the called of God: "called of God an high priest" — divinely designated to the office; the called of God; the designated; the the office; the appointed; the divine. The expositors observe Gill's note: Gill notes the resemblance between Melchizedek and Christ; the the resemblance; the Melchizedek; the Christ; the agree; the like. The witnesses press the order of Melchisedec: "after the order of Melchisedec" — after the higher, royal, abiding order; the order of Melchisedec; the higher; the royal; the abiding; the not Aaron. The expositors draw the lesson on the resemblances fulfilled: the resemblances to that king-priest are fulfilled in Christ; the resemblances fulfilled; the the king-priest; the in Christ; the fulfilled; the agree. The witnesses observe the seals v.6: this verse seals the theme of v.6; the seals v.6; the the theme; the returns; the repeated; the the title. The expositors note the must rebuke first: Paul must first rebuke their dullness before expounding; the must rebuke first; the their dullness; the before expounding; the the rebuke; the the theme. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the called of God an high priest, the after the order of Melchisedec, and the Melchizedek priesthood. The verse thus records Christ's designation to the Melchizedek priesthood — teaching that Christ was divinely called to the high priesthood after the superior order of Melchizedek.
11:
"Of whom we have many things to say, and hard to be uttered, seeing ye are dull of hearing." The witnesses note the difficulty of expounding deep things to dull hearers. Gill notes Paul has many things to say of Melchizedek, or of Christ, or of his priesthood — as he does at length in the following chapters — but they are "hard to be uttered," not in themselves, but because of the hearers. The witnesses note Paul saying that he has many things to say, hard to be uttered, because they are dull of hearing; the deep truths hindered not by their difficulty but by the hearers' dullness — that Paul, about to launch into the great Melchizedek theme, checks himself to deal with his readers' condition: "of whom we have many things to say" (much rich teaching about Melchizedek and Christ's priesthood) "and hard to be uttered" — and Paul names the true difficulty: not that the truths are too obscure in themselves, but "seeing ye are dull of hearing," the hearers had grown sluggish and slow in spiritual understanding, so that the deep things could not easily be made plain to them; so that the fault lay not in the doctrine but in their dull, untaught state; of whom we have many things to say, and hard to be uttered. The witnesses note of whom we have many things to say, and hard to be uttered. The witnesses note seeing ye are dull of hearing.

The witnesses note the deep truths hindered by the hearers' dullness — many rich things to say, hard to utter, not for their obscurity but because the hearers had grown slow of understanding.

So the witnesses make the verse the deep truths hindered by dull hearing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the difficulty lay not in the doctrine but in the hearers' sluggish, untaught state. The witnesses note the many things: "of whom we have many things to say" — Gill notes much teaching of Melchizedek and Christ's priesthood; the many things; the much teaching; the Melchizedek; the the priesthood; the rich. The expositors observe the hard to utter: "and hard to be uttered" — hard to make plain; the hard to utter; the hard; the to make plain; the difficult; the the truths. The witnesses press the not in themselves: not that the truths are too obscure in themselves; the not in themselves; the not obscure; the not the doctrine; the the truths; the clear. The expositors draw the lesson on the dull of hearing: "seeing ye are dull of hearing" — the hearers grown sluggish and slow; the dull of hearing; the sluggish; the slow; the the hearers; the untaught. The witnesses observe the could not be made plain: so the deep things could not easily be made plain to them; the could not be made plain; the the deep things; the not easily; the to them; the hindered. The expositors note the fault in them: the fault lay in their dull state, not the doctrine; the fault in them; the their dull state; the not the doctrine; the the hearers; the slow. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of whom we have many things to say and hard to be uttered, the seeing ye are dull of hearing, and the deep truths hindered. The verse thus records the deep truths hindered by dull hearing — teaching that the difficulty lay not in the doctrine but in the hearers' sluggish, untaught state.
12:
"For when for the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God; and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat." The witnesses note the rebuke for spiritual backwardness. Gill notes these Hebrews had great advantages — descended from Abraham, having the law and the Old Testament writings, and some having enjoyed the ministry of Christ and the apostles, it being now about thirty years from the day of Pentecost. The witnesses note Paul rebuking that, when they ought to be teachers, they need to be taught again the first principles, having need of milk not strong meat; the reproof of arrested spiritual growth — that Paul charges them with a shameful backwardness: "when for the time ye ought to be teachers" (after so long in the faith, they should have grown able to instruct others) "ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God" — they had to be re-taught the very ABC of the faith — "and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat"; the witnesses note the figure — milk being the elementary, easy doctrines fit for babes, and "strong meat" the deeper truths fit for the mature — so that, instead of advancing, they had slipped back to spiritual infancy, needing milk when they should have been feeding on solid food; ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles of the oracles of God. The witnesses note when for the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again. The witnesses note are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat.

The witnesses note the rebuke for arrested growth — when they ought to be teachers, needing to be re-taught the first principles; slipped back to infancy, needing milk not strong meat.

So the witnesses make the verse the reproof of arrested spiritual growth. The lesson the expositors draw is that, instead of advancing to maturity, they had slipped back to needing the elementary truths. The witnesses note the ought to be teachers: "for the time ye ought to be teachers" — Gill notes after so long they should instruct others; the ought to be teachers; the after so long; the instruct; the others; the advanced. The expositors observe the advantages: they had great advantages, the law, the apostles' ministry; the the advantages; the the law; the the apostles; the thirty years; the privileged. The witnesses press the taught again: "ye have need that one teach you again which be the first principles" — re-taught the ABC of the faith; the taught again; the the first principles; the the ABC; the re-taught; the elementary. The expositors draw the lesson on the milk: "have need of milk, and not of strong meat" — milk the easy doctrines for babes; the milk; the easy doctrines; the for babes; the elementary; the not meat. The witnesses observe the strong meat: strong meat the deeper truths for the mature; the strong meat; the deeper truths; the the mature; the solid; the meat. The expositors note the slipped back: instead of advancing, they slipped back to infancy; the slipped back; the not advancing; the infancy; the backward; the milk. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when for the time ye ought to be teachers ye have need that one teach you again, the are become such as have need of milk and not of strong meat, and the arrested growth. The verse thus records the reproof of arrested spiritual growth — teaching that, instead of advancing, they had slipped back to needing the elementary truths.
13:
"For every one that useth milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness: for he is a babe." The witnesses note that those still on milk are unskilled babes in the word. Gill notes everyone that useth milk — and sits down contented with the first principles of the gospel, easily taken in and digested, or makes use of the ceremonial law as a schoolmaster — is unskilful in the deeper things. The witnesses note Paul declaring that everyone who uses milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness, for he is a babe; the spiritual infancy of those who feed only on first principles — that Paul explains the figure: "every one that useth milk" (whoever can take in only the simplest, most elementary teaching) "is unskilful in the word of righteousness" — unpractised, inexpert, lacking discernment in the deeper doctrine of the gospel (the word that reveals the righteousness of God in Christ) — and Paul gives the reason: "for he is a babe," a spiritual infant, undeveloped and untaught; so that to remain content with milk is to remain a babe, unfit for and unskilled in the weightier truths of the faith; for every one that useth milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness. The witnesses note every one that useth milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness. The witnesses note for he is a babe.

The witnesses note the spiritual infancy of those who feed only on milk — unskilful in the word of righteousness, mere babes, unfit for and unpractised in the deeper doctrine.

So the witnesses make the verse the spiritual infancy of the milk-fed. The lesson the expositors draw is that to remain content with milk is to remain a babe, unskilled in the deeper word of righteousness. The witnesses note the useth milk: "every one that useth milk" — Gill notes whoever takes in only the simplest teaching; the useth milk; the simplest; the elementary; the first principles; the easy. The expositors observe the unskilful: "is unskilful in the word of righteousness" — unpractised, inexpert in the deeper doctrine; the unskilful; the unpractised; the inexpert; the the deeper doctrine; the lacking. The witnesses press the word of righteousness: the word that reveals God's righteousness in Christ; the word of righteousness; the God's righteousness; the in Christ; the the gospel; the the doctrine. The expositors draw the lesson on the a babe: "for he is a babe" — a spiritual infant, undeveloped; the a babe; the spiritual infant; the undeveloped; the untaught; the infancy. The witnesses observe the remain content: to remain content with milk is to remain a babe; the remain content; the with milk; the remain a babe; the unfit; the infancy. The expositors note the unfit: unfit for and unskilled in the weightier truths; the unfit; the unskilled; the the weightier truths; the the faith; the babe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the every one that useth milk is unskilful in the word of righteousness, the for he is a babe, and the spiritual infancy. The verse thus records the spiritual infancy of the milk-fed — teaching that to remain content with milk is to remain a babe, unskilled in the deeper word of righteousness.
14:
"But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil." The witnesses note that strong meat belongs to the mature, whose discernment is trained. Gill notes "strong meat" belongs to them that are of full age, or perfect — not a perfection of justification (for babes in Christ are as perfectly justified as the grown), but a maturity of spiritual growth and understanding. The witnesses note Paul declaring that strong meat belongs to the mature, who by use have their senses exercised to discern good and evil; the deeper truths fit for the spiritually mature and discerning — that Paul completes the figure with its bright side: "strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age" (the deeper, weightier doctrines of the gospel are the proper food of the spiritually mature, the grown men in Christ) — "even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil"; the witnesses note the mark of maturity here — not mere age, but a trained spiritual discernment, gained "by reason of use" (by habitual practice and exercise of the faculties of the soul), so that the mature can tell sound from unsound, truth from error, good from evil; so that growth in grace is shown by an exercised, discerning judgment, fit to feed on the solid food of the deeper truths; their senses exercised to discern both good and evil. The witnesses note strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age. The witnesses note who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.

The witnesses note the deeper truths fit for the mature — whose senses are exercised by use to discern good and evil; growth shown by a trained, discerning judgment, fit for solid food.

So the witnesses make the verse the deeper truths fit for the discerning mature. The lesson the expositors draw is that strong meat belongs to the spiritually mature, whose discernment is trained by habitual use. The witnesses note the strong meat: "strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age" — Gill notes the deeper doctrines for the mature; the strong meat; the the deeper doctrines; the the mature; the full age; the grown. The expositors observe the not justification: maturity of growth, not a perfection of justification; the not justification; the maturity; the growth; the understanding; the perfect. The witnesses press the by reason of use: "by reason of use" — by habitual practice and exercise; the by reason of use; the habitual; the practice; the exercise; the trained. The expositors draw the lesson on the senses exercised: "have their senses exercised" — the soul's faculties trained; the senses exercised; the the faculties; the trained; the the soul; the practised. The witnesses observe the discern good and evil: "to discern both good and evil" — to tell sound from unsound, truth from error; the discern good and evil; the sound from unsound; the truth from error; the good from evil; the judge. The expositors note the growth shown: growth in grace is shown by a discerning judgment; the growth shown; the in grace; the discerning judgment; the the mature; the trained. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, the who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil, and the deeper truths for the mature. The verse thus records the deeper truths fit for the discerning mature — teaching that strong meat belongs to the spiritually mature, whose discernment is trained by habitual use.
